title: You Bear Guilt For What You Allow
author: Sam Riley
orientation: A prose-poem indictment by Sam Riley arguing that anyone who tolerates legal abortion shares moral guilt for it, naming specific complicit groups (police, pastors, Christians, judges, legislators, mothers) and rejecting incremental, court-based, or election-based passivity as faithless delay.
audiences: Pro-life Christian who votes Republican and trusts the courts
Pastor weighing whether to preach against abortion from the pulpit
State legislator who claims a pro-life label but won't pass an abolition bill
Skeptical secular reader trying to understand abolitionist rhetoric
Abolitionist activist looking for moral language to confront neighbors

questions.question: What is the central moral claim Sam Riley is making in this piece?
questions.audience: Skeptical secular reader trying to understand abolitionist rhetoric
questions.answer: Riley's thesis is that passive toleration of abortion is itself a form of participation in it. He treats inaction by citizens, officials, and Christians as morally indistinguishable from the act of killing, because the system of legal abortion only continues with their consent.
questions.quote: You uphold the system! You allow the murder! Your hands are as bloody as theirs!

questions.question: Why does Riley think a sincere pro-life sentiment isn't enough?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who votes Republican and trusts the courts
questions.answer: Because feelings that don't translate into the actual abolition of abortion accomplish nothing for the children being killed or, in his framing, for God. Emotion without effective action is merely evidence of one's humanity, not a discharge of moral duty.
questions.quote: You can feel deep hatred in your soul when you hear about abortion and know that it is a horrible injustice, which shows your humanity, but if it doesn't result in abolition what good is it to anyone?

questions.question: Which specific groups does Riley name as complicit in abortion?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for moral language to confront neighbors
questions.answer: He lists a chain of enablers: police, delivery drivers, pastors, ordinary Christians, abortionists' customers and neighbors, mothers seeking abortions, judges bound by precedent, and legislators who refuse to act. The breadth of the list is itself the argument: complicity is widespread, not confined to the abortion provider.
questions.quote: Police officers who allow it to continue, delivery men supply weapons on command, pastors who fail to denounce it, Christians who won't take a stand, abortionists eat at your businesses, mothers fill the demand, judges rule unjustly by precedent, legislators held back by their hand!

questions.question: How does Riley respond to calls for patience and incremental progress?
questions.audience: State legislator who claims a pro-life label but won't pass an abolition bill
questions.answer: He treats appeals to patience as a pathetic excuse offered while children continue to die. For Riley there is no morally legitimate timeline of delay; justice for victims of homicide cannot be deferred to a politically convenient future.
questions.quote: "Patience! Peace!" You pathetically cry as children are fed to the feds. The time for justice is always now.

questions.question: What does the article say about pinning hopes on Trump or Amy Coney Barrett to end abortion?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who votes Republican and trusts the courts
questions.answer: Riley dismisses it as 'passing the buck'—a way to volunteer politicians as a substitute for personal obedience. He frames the posture as cowardly outsourcing of a duty that scripture lays on the reader directly.
questions.quote: "Amy Coney Barrett will do it, Trump will do it, there they are Lord, send them, not me." How long can you pass the buck?

questions.question: What kind of action does Riley call readers to instead of trusting courts and political compromise?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for moral language to confront neighbors
questions.answer: He calls for ending compromise, abandoning hope in human courts and creeds, appealing instead to divine authority, and treating the neighbor-love command with the seriousness of someone facing personal judgment ('act like you will get the axe').
questions.quote: End the slaughter, stop your compromise, destroy your hope in courts and creeds and appeal to the divine Court and the divine Creed. Love your neighbor as yourself and act like you will get the axe!

questions.question: What religious accusation does Riley level at silent Christians and pastors?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing whether to preach against abortion from the pulpit
questions.answer: He accuses them of idolatry—comparing forty-seven years of legal abortion to burning incense to foreign gods, and likening silent religious people to a group bowing to Baal. Silence in the face of child-killing is, in his framing, a form of false worship.
questions.quote: Forty-seven years are in the hole where we dumped the children, forty-seven years of burning fetal incense to foreign gods.

questions.question: Why does Riley use such graphic, confrontational language?
questions.audience: Skeptical secular reader trying to understand abolitionist rhetoric
questions.answer: He argues that people refuse to recognize the reality of abortion until they are forced to confront the physical horror of it. The graphic register is a deliberate rhetorical attempt to strip away the abstraction that makes complicity comfortable.
questions.quote: Must we sift through the remains of their bloody carcasses before we can say we created the hell we live in?

questions.question: How does Riley characterize people who try to soften or moderate abolitionist demands?
questions.audience: State legislator who claims a pro-life label but won't pass an abolition bill
questions.answer: He says they ask for regulated killing rather than its abolition, give legal cover to murder, and brand consistent abolitionists as extremists in order to stay socially comfortable. In his framing, moderation is itself the mechanism by which the killing is preserved.
questions.quote: Your demands are for sharper knives for butchers and not the abolition of the slaughter! You frame iniquity by statute and give approval to the killer! You call those who want it ended extremists and you hastily disavow!

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Develops the same distinction Riley draws between mere pro-life sentiment and actually demanding the abolition of abortion.

related.title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Sharpens the religious version of the indictment: faith requires more than the pro-life label Riley says is insufficient.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the policy-level case against the gradualism Riley denounces as compromise with murder.

related.title: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
related.reason: Engages the scriptural defense often used to justify the very passivity Riley calls bloodguilt.

related.title: What the Still, Small Voice Said: A Parable for Churches Who Won't Work For Abolition
related.reason: Extends Riley's charge against silent pastors and congregations into narrative form.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Another Sam Riley piece naming the 'compromise' posture this article rejects, aimed at prominent pro-life leaders.

related.title: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
related.reason: Riley's direct defense of the confrontational tone he uses here against accusations of extremism.
